title: Treasury warns of inflation surge, growth slide
full_text:
Australia faces the prospect of inflation surging to 7.25 per cent and negative economic growth towards the end of the year in a worst-case scenario where Donald Trump’s war in Iran continues to pile pressure on global oil prices and disrupt supply chains.
In Treasury’s worst-case scenario where global oil prices peak as high as $US200 ($277) per barrel in the September quarter, inflation skyrockets through 7 per cent, real GDP takes a 0.5 per cent hit through 2026-27 and 2027-28, and unemployment peaks at almost 5 per cent in two years’ time.
However, in Treasury’s base case, inflation is expected to halve next year to 2.5 per cent on the assumption that oil prices ease, returning the forecast in line with last year’s budget expectations that inflation would return to within the Reserve Bank’s target band.
Brent crude was last trading at $US104 as US President Trump said the ceasefire between the US and Iran was on “massive life support” following Iran’s latest counter-proposal to end hostilities, which he called “simply unacceptable”, and was unlikely to make significant progress until after the President meets Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping later this week.
In the government’s base case, the economy is expected to slow to 1.75 per cent next year as pressures from the Middle East oil shock flow through to households and weigh on spending, revised down significantly from Treasury’s expectations of 2.5 per cent growth in last year’s budget and MYEFO.
Wealth editor Julie-anne Sprague on why the big changes exclude one key generation.
The government also dramatically revised its forecasts for inflation, predicting price growth would peak in the middle of this year at 5 per cent, up from the 3 per cent forecast in last year’s budget.
However, in the base case, inflation is expected to halve next year to 2.5 per cent on the assumption that oil prices ease, returning the forecast in line with last year’s budget expectations that inflation would return to within the Reserve Bank’s target band.
Treasury estimated that inflation would have been sitting at 4 per cent without pressures from the Iran war – still well outside the RBA’s target of between 2 and 3 per cent – driven by the roll-off of government electricity rebates, persistent price pressures and supply constraints in the economy.
RBA governor Michele Bullock last week warned that government handouts to households amid an economic crisis sparked by the Iran war will make it harder to temper inflation, as the RBA lifted interest rates for the third consecutive meeting.
“When governments are spending a lot of money and we’re running up against capacity constraints, then they do need to think about whether or not there’s ways they can help the inflation problem by looking for ways to constrain demand,” Ms Bullock said at her post-RBA meeting press conference.
“That’s one thing that they can do. All I’m saying is that the extent to which government make up the shortfalls for households by giving them more money, it makes it harder to dampen demand.”
Ms Bullock said this was essential in the fight against inflation.
The government has revised down its forecasts for growth in public demand this year, from 3 per cent in the last budget and MYEFO, to 2.75 per cent in this year’s budget papers, though it raised its expectations for government spending next year, keeping public demand at 2.75 per cent, up from the 2 per cent forecast in the last budget.
Treasury also revised upwards its expectations for business investment, forecasting 2.5 per cent growth nest year up from the meagre 1.5 per cent growth expected in last year’s budget.
“A strong pipeline of data centre and renewables projects is expected to support underlying growth in non-mining investment. This momentum is expected to continue, with non-mining investment forecast to grow by 5 per cent in 2025-26, 3 per cent in 2026-27 and 2.5 per cent in 2027-28,” the budget papers said.
Private sector demand, which includes business investment, grew by half that of government spending in the last quarter of the National Accounts. Public demand – government spending and investment – grew by 0.9 per cent in the quarter, while private demand was up 0.4 per cent.
Treasury has jettisoned hopes of achieving its productivity growth forecasts in the next three years, with budget papers pushing out projections for realising its modest ambition of 1.2 per cent growth until 2031 at the earliest.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
Document AUSTOL0020260512em5c002h3
summary: Australia's Treasury warns of potential inflation surging to 7.25% and negative GDP growth due to ongoing geopolitical tensions, while a more optimistic scenario predicts inflation halving to 2.5% next year.
author: MATTHEW CRANSTON , THOMAS HENRY
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Worst-case scenario predicts inflation at 7.25% and GDP decline of 0.5% amid oil price spikes.
Base case forecasts inflation to drop to 2.5% next year if oil prices ease.
RBA governor warns that government handouts could exacerbate inflation challenges.
relevance: Understanding inflation and economic growth forecasts is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals in risk assessment and financial planning.
